title: Black Panther Party
summary: A revolutionary Black liberation organization founded in 1966 in Oakland that used community programs, armed self‑defense, and a radical critique of American racism and capitalism.

title: Origins of Judaism
summary: One of the oldest monotheistic traditions, emerging among the ancient Israelites of Canaan and shaped by covenant, Torah, exile, and the rise and fall of the Temple in Jerusalem.

title: Origins of Christianity
summary: Christianity began as a Jewish movement in 1st‑century Palestine, centered on Jesus of Nazareth and the belief that he was the crucified and risen Messiah. From this small group of Jewish disciples, it grew into a diverse, increasingly Gentile faith that spread across the Roman Empire.

title: Origins of Islam
summary: Islam emerged in 7th-century Arabia through the Prophet Muhammad, who received revelations from God that became the Quran. From a small community in Mecca, it grew into a faith and civilization that spread rapidly across the Middle East, North Africa, and beyond.

title: The Start of World War I
summary: World War I began in 1914 after the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand triggered the July Crisis, a chain of alliances, ultimatums, mobilizations, and declarations of war. What started as a conflict between Austria-Hungary and Serbia quickly spread into a continent-wide war.

title: The Start of World War II
summary: World War II began in 1939 after Nazi Germany invaded Poland, following years of aggressive expansion and failed appeasement by Britain and France. The invasion triggered declarations of war and turned a European crisis into a global conflict.

title: The Solar System
summary: The Solar System is the Sun and all the objects that orbit it, including eight planets, dwarf planets, moons, asteroids, and comets. It formed about 4.6 billion years ago from a cloud of gas and dust that collapsed into a spinning disk.

title: Nutrition Label Literacy and Food Marketing
summary: Nutrition labels can be technically accurate while still misleading shoppers through serving sizes, front-of-package claims, and marketing language. Understanding labels helps consumers see the difference between a product’s presentation and its actual nutritional profile.

title: The Periodic Table
summary: The periodic table organizes all known elements by atomic number and reveals repeating patterns in their properties. It helps explain how elements are grouped, why they behave similarly, and how periodic trends predict chemical behavior.

title: Ida B. Wells
summary: Ida B. Wells was a journalist, anti-lynching activist, suffragist, and civil rights leader who used investigation and public writing to expose racial violence and fight injustice. Her work helped shape the Black freedom struggle in the United States.

title: Fractions
summary: Fractions describe parts of a whole and are used in everyday math, measurement, and comparison. They also connect to decimals, percentages, and algebra.

title: Venice: The City on the Water
summary: Venice is a city built on a lagoon in northeastern Italy, founded by refugees seeking safety in the marshes and transformed over centuries into one of the most powerful trading empires in the world.

title: How Inflation Works
summary: Inflation is the general rise in prices over time, which means money buys less than it used to. It is measured with price indexes like the CPI and is influenced by demand, supply, and policy.

title: Mental Math
summary: Mental math is the skill of doing calculations in your head without a calculator. It uses strategies like rounding, breaking numbers apart, and spotting patterns.

title: What Are Interest Rates
summary: Interest rates are the cost of borrowing money or the reward for saving it. They shape loans, mortgages, credit cards, savings accounts, and the broader economy.

title: How Banks Make Money
summary: Banks make money primarily by charging more interest on loans than they pay on deposits. They also earn fees, process transactions, and use your deposits to fund lending at scale.

title: Time Value of Money
summary: Money today is worth more than the same amount in the future because it can be invested, earn interest, and avoid uncertainty. Time value of money explains present value, future value, and discounting.

title: Spanish: Greetings and Introductions
summary: Learn to greet people, introduce yourself, and start basic conversations in Spanish using practical phrases and everyday patterns.

title: Spanish: Pronunciation and Sounding Words Out
summary: Learn the Spanish alphabet, vowel sounds, stress rules, and spelling patterns so you can pronounce new words with confidence.

title: Chemical Bonding and Compounds
summary: Atoms bond together to form compounds, and the type of bond determines almost everything about a substance — its structure, stability, melting point, and how it reacts. This topic explains ionic, covalent, and metallic bonding, and how compounds are named and classified.

title: Atoms, Molecules, and Cells
summary: Atoms are the smallest units of matter that retain an element's identity, molecules are groups of atoms bonded together, and cells are the smallest units of life built from molecules. This topic shows how matter is organized from the chemical level to the biological level.

title: Genghis Khan and the Mongol Empire
summary: Genghis Khan united the Mongol tribes in the early 1200s and built the largest contiguous land empire in history. The Mongol Empire reshaped warfare, trade, and political power across Eurasia.

title: The Great Wall of China
summary: The Great Wall of China was built and rebuilt over many centuries as a northern defense system against invasions, especially under the Qin and Ming dynasties. It became one of the most famous symbols of Chinese history, state power, and engineering.

title: Relationships and Attachment
summary: Explores why people bond, how those bonds develop and break down, and what psychological forces shape attraction, trust, jealousy, conflict, and long-term connection.

title: Aggression and Conflict
summary: Examines why people become hostile, defensive, and violent — from the frustration of blocked goals and misread threats to status conflicts, deindividuation in crowds, and the dynamics that turn minor friction into serious harm.

title: Stoicism — What Can You Actually Control?
summary: A practical ancient philosophy built on one foundational question: what is genuinely within your control, and what is not? From Epictetus to Marcus Aurelius, Stoicism offers tools for equanimity, resilience, and purposeful action that are as applicable today as they were in ancient Rome.

title: The Cold War — How the World Split in Two
summary: The Cold War was a global rivalry between the United States and the Soviet Union that reshaped politics, war, technology, and daily life without turning into a direct full-scale war between them. From the ruins of World War II to the collapse of the Soviet Union, it divided the world into competing blocs defined by ideology, nuclear fear, proxy wars, and struggles for influence.

title: The Israeli-Palestinian Conflict
summary: The Israeli-Palestinian conflict grew out of competing national movements, imperial rule, and conflicting promises made during and after World War I. Over more than a century, it has passed through phases of partition, war, occupation, uprisings, failed peace efforts, and repeated Gaza wars, leaving deep scars on both societies and shaping politics across the Middle East and the wider world.

title: The Panama Canal: How a Shortcut Reshaped Trade and Power
summary: The Panama Canal turned a narrow strip of Central America into a shortcut that reshaped global trade routes and great‑power strategy. From failed French schemes and U.S. control of the Canal Zone to Panamanian sovereignty, expansion for megaships, and climate‑strained water supplies, its story shows how engineering, empire, and environmental change can converge in a single chokepoint.

title: Beer for Beginners
summary: Beer menus and bar conversations are easier to navigate than they look once you understand what beer is made of, how styles differ, and how to describe what you like. This topic takes a complete beginner from ingredients and families through flavor reading, serving formats, and confident ordering.

title: Wine for Beginners
summary: Wine lists and wine conversations become far less intimidating once you understand what wine is made of, how the main families differ, and how to describe what you like. This topic takes a complete beginner from grape to glass, covering ingredients, style families, flavor reading, serving conditions, and confident ordering.
